name: Optimize Ard bytecode VM against PR #93 Go-backend benchmark suite

description: Baseline setup: added autoresearch harness around the full PR #93 runtime benchmark corpus and recorded starting VM total.

asi.hypothesis: Establish a stable full-suite runtime baseline before touching VM code.
asi.insight: Decode/fs/sql are already relatively close to Go; the biggest headroom is in interpreter-heavy pure workloads, which points to dispatch/object/frame overhead rather than external IO.
asi.next_focus: Inspect hot VM operations in vm.go and object allocation behavior in runtime/object.go.

description: Rejected VM function-name map lookup: duplicate function names across modules likely changed `Run("main")` resolution and made benchmarks terminate unrealistically fast.

asi.hypothesis: Replace linear function-name lookup with a map and preallocate frame stacks to reduce dispatch/allocation overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The name->index map changed function resolution semantics when names were duplicated across modules, producing impossibly fast benchmark results and likely running the wrong entrypoint.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep only semantics-preserving allocation improvements; avoid global function-name maps unless keyed by a unique symbol/module-qualified name.
asi.surprising_signal: All benchmark runtimes collapsed to ~4 ms, which is a strong sign of semantic breakage rather than a real speedup.

description: Discarded stack/frame slice preallocation in VM: harmless but no measurable full-suite gain against noise.

asi.hypothesis: Preallocating frame stack capacity would reduce slice growth and improve interpreter-heavy workloads.
asi.rollback_reason: The aggregate metric was slightly worse/flat; any savings from fewer stack growth allocations are below noise or offset elsewhere.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on per-op object allocation and dispatch overhead instead of slice capacity tuning.
asi.learning: Current VM slowdown is unlikely to be dominated by stack slice growth.

description: Interned immutable bool objects and cached small integer objects in runtime.Object factories to cut VM allocation churn across interpreter-heavy workloads.

asi.hypothesis: A large share of VM overhead is repeated allocation of tiny immutable primitive objects; interning bools and common small ints should reduce GC and allocation pressure broadly.
asi.learning: This is a real cross-suite win: decode improved, pure workloads improved a lot, and IO-heavy workloads stayed roughly flat. Primitive allocation churn was a major bottleneck.
asi.next_action_hint: Look for the next tier of hot primitive/object churn, especially float/string construction, comparison/arithmetic dispatch, and avoidable copies.
asi.microbenchmark_signal: BenchmarkBytecodeRun dropped from ~5.22ms / 11.39MB / 197k allocs to ~3.56ms / 5.09MB / 98.6k allocs.

description: Discarded composite checker type memoization: minor microbenchmark gain but worse full-suite runtime, especially decode and shape-heavy paths.

asi.hypothesis: Memoizing checker composite type wrappers would remove repeated type metadata allocation during runtime object construction.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark suite regressed materially versus the current best despite a tiny microbenchmark improvement, likely because sync.Map overhead outweighed saved allocations on realistic workloads.
asi.next_action_hint: Prefer zero-overhead fast paths in hot VM/runtime operations over global concurrent caches.
asi.learning: Microbenchmark allocation wins can be misleading when they introduce lookup/synchronization overhead into every runtime constructor call.

description: Discarded inlined arithmetic/comparison fast paths in the VM loop: some benchmarks improved, but aggregate runtime stayed worse than the current best primitive-interning change.

asi.hypothesis: Inlining common arithmetic/comparison cases directly in the dispatch loop would cut helper-call and repeated kind-switch overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The suite remained slower than the current best; any dispatch savings were offset by larger switch bodies / branch effects and did not beat the allocation win already landed.
asi.next_action_hint: Target allocation-heavy constructors or copies next, especially strings/floats/collections and copy-heavy opcodes.
asi.learning: Dispatch overhead matters, but primitive object allocation was the bigger win so far.

description: Discarded primitive-sharing in Object.Copy(): it improved some workloads but regressed decode/shape enough to lose badly against the current best.

asi.hypothesis: OpCopy may allocate many redundant primitive objects; sharing immutable primitive copies could lower churn.
asi.rollback_reason: Realistic workloads regressed substantially, especially decode/shape, so pointer sharing in Copy did not translate into a suite-level win.
asi.next_action_hint: Investigate collection/struct/method-heavy paths rather than Copy alone; also consider whether benchmark variation in decode is masking smaller effects.
asi.learning: Not all primitive allocations are from Copy; the bigger broad win remains constructor interning for ints/bools.

description: Reused VM frames and their locals/stack backing slices across calls to cut per-call allocation churn in the interpreter.

asi.hypothesis: A large remaining VM cost is allocating fresh frames, locals slices, and growing stack slices on every call; reusing them within a VM run should reduce call-heavy workload overhead.
asi.learning: This is a real second-tier win after primitive interning. The bytecode microbenchmark collapsed from ~3.56ms / 5.09MB / 98.6k allocs to ~1.98ms / 17KB / 159 allocs, and the full suite improved to ~830.6ms.
asi.next_action_hint: With constructor and frame churn reduced, focus next on collection/method-heavy operations and any remaining high-frequency object construction in decode/string processing.
asi.note: One prior rerun of the same change was noisier (~871.5ms); the confirmation run came in materially faster and beats the current best.

description: Switched the VM operand stack from append/reslice semantics to a fixed backing slice with an explicit stack pointer, reducing hot push/pop overhead on top of frame reuse.

asi.hypothesis: After frame reuse, push/pop still sit hot in the profile; replacing slice append/reslice with an explicit stack pointer should shave dispatch overhead across many opcodes.
asi.learning: This stacks cleanly with frame reuse and primitive interning. The full suite improved again to ~818.1ms, with especially strong decode and shape gains.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile with the new baseline; likely next targets are evalBinary/evalCompare/pop fast paths, SetRefinedType, and collection/string-heavy helpers.
asi.microbenchmark_signal: BenchmarkBytecodeRun remains around ~2.0ms with only ~17KB / 159 allocs per op, confirming allocation pressure is now drastically lower.

description: Discarded a larger small-int cache window (`[-1,4096]`): it reduced microbenchmark allocations further but did not beat the current full-suite best.

asi.hypothesis: A wider small-int cache would catch more loop counters/indices/sizes and reduce remaining MakeInt allocations.
asi.rollback_reason: The full suite stayed slightly slower than the current best despite lower microbenchmark alloc counts, so the larger cache did not provide enough real benefit.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the narrower cache and target remaining time hotspots instead of chasing more static interning.
asi.learning: Remaining bottlenecks are no longer primarily raw allocation count in the microbenchmark.

description: Discarded lightweight child-VM spawning for closures/fibers: it helped async/sql paths somewhat but did not beat the current full-suite best.

asi.hypothesis: Avoid rebuilding VM registries/maps when spawning child VMs for closures and fibers by constructing a lightweight child around shared immutable state.
asi.rollback_reason: The aggregate suite remained slightly slower than the current best, so the closure/fiber spawn savings were too narrow to justify keeping the change.
asi.next_action_hint: Look for optimizations that help the dominant decode/sales/shape paths, not just async-heavy or closure-heavy slices of the suite.
asi.learning: Child-VM setup is not the dominant remaining cost across the benchmark corpus.

description: Added fast-path exits in Object.SetRefinedType so already-stable values skip repeated refinement and kind/name recomputation.

asi.hypothesis: With allocator pressure reduced, redundant type-refinement work becomes visible; most values should be able to skip SetRefinedType entirely once already concrete.
asi.learning: This was another strong broad win, especially for decode-heavy paths. Skipping unnecessary refinement work dropped the suite to ~788.5ms and decode to ~358.2ms.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile from this new baseline. Remaining likely targets are evalBinary/evalCompare, newFrame bookkeeping, and collection/string helper paths.
asi.microbenchmark_signal: BenchmarkBytecodeRun improved further to ~1.93ms/op with allocation counts essentially unchanged, confirming the gain is CPU-side rather than alloc-side.

description: Discarded lazy stack-slot clearing on pop: a small microbenchmark gain, but the full suite was still slower than the current best.

asi.hypothesis: Removing the per-pop nil write and clearing stack slots only at frame recycle would reduce hot pop overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The suite stayed slower than the current best despite a tiny microbenchmark improvement, so the tradeoff was not worthwhile.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep per-pop clearing as-is and continue after higher-leverage CPU hotspots like evalCompare/evalBinary or typeFor-heavy paths.
asi.learning: Some micro hot-spot wins are too small or too workload-specific to beat the current suite best.

description: Discarded prebuilt TypeID->name lookup tables in the VM: small lookup simplification, but still slower than the current best full-suite state.

asi.hypothesis: Linear scans over Program.Types inside typeFor/typeNameFor were still visible in profiles; replacing them with a prebuilt TypeID->name table might reduce that overhead broadly.
asi.rollback_reason: The change failed to beat the current suite best and even worsened the VM microbenchmark slightly, so the extra map traffic is not paying off.
asi.next_action_hint: Target higher-cost CPU hotspots like evalCompare/pop/newFrame rather than replacing one small lookup with another map lookup.
asi.learning: typeFor time is probably secondary or already mostly hidden by the existing per-VM cache.

description: Discarded narrower helper-level cleanup in evalBinary/evalCompare: slightly less repeated kind work, but still not enough to beat the current best suite result.

asi.hypothesis: Reducing repeated Kind()/generic equality work inside evalBinary/evalCompare might capture part of the remaining compare/arithmetic CPU cost without growing the main dispatch loop.
asi.rollback_reason: The change still failed to beat the current best full-suite result, so the local helper cleanup is too small to matter at suite level.
asi.next_action_hint: Look beyond tiny helper cleanups—consider reducing frame setup work further or targeting opcode-specific hot paths in decode-heavy execution.
asi.learning: Comparison/arithmetic overhead remains visible in microprofiles, but broad suite wins likely require a more structural reduction than helper-level tidy-ups.

description: Discarded removal of duplicate local-slice clearing in newFrame: tiny CPU win locally, but still not enough to beat the current best suite result.

asi.hypothesis: Reused frames are already zeroed in recycleFrame, so clearing locals again in newFrame is redundant work that can be removed safely.
asi.rollback_reason: Even though the microbenchmark nudged down, the full suite still did not beat the current best, so the duplicate-clear removal is too small to keep on its own.
asi.next_action_hint: Search for broader wins in decode-heavy execution or larger structural reductions around comparisons/type handling rather than shaving isolated frame bookkeeping.
asi.learning: The remaining newFrame cost is not dominated by this particular redundant clear.

description: Discarded primitive fast paths in makeValueWithType: plausible for unwrap-heavy code, but the full suite regressed badly and did not beat the current best.

asi.hypothesis: Maybe/Result unwrap and related method paths often materialize primitive values; specializing makeValueWithType for primitive target types could reuse primitive constructor fast paths.
asi.rollback_reason: The suite regressed heavily, with especially noisy/regressed fs and word-frequency behavior, so this did not translate into a reliable broad win.
asi.next_action_hint: Avoid speculative specialization in low-frequency helper paths; focus on consistently hot interpreter/core paths or update autoresearch.md with latest dead ends.
asi.learning: makeValueWithType is not a dominant enough source of overhead to justify extra branching here.

description: Removed per-instruction debug bookkeeping from the VM interpreter loop so successful executions no longer update lastOp/lastIP/lastFn on every opcode.

asi.hypothesis: The interpreter was doing pure bookkeeping work every opcode to support a rare stack-underflow error path; removing those writes should produce a broad CPU-side win.
asi.learning: This was a strong broad win. The suite dropped to ~773.2ms, with improvements across nearly every benchmark, and the bytecode microbenchmark improved to ~1.82ms/op without changing allocation counts.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile again from this new baseline; remaining likely targets are pop/evalCompare/newFrame and other always-on per-op costs, not low-frequency helper specializations.
asi.tradeoff: Stack underflow errors are now less detailed, but runtime semantics and correctness checks remain intact.

description: Used unchecked stack pops in verified hot opcode paths so arithmetic, compare, jump, local, and simple stack ops avoid checked-pop error overhead in the interpreter loop.

asi.hypothesis: The verifier guarantees stack discipline for valid bytecode, so hot opcode paths can use unchecked pops and leave the checked path only for less frequent/general operations.
asi.learning: This produced another broad win. The suite dropped to ~768.0ms and the microbenchmark improved to ~1.71ms/op with unchanged allocations, confirming the gain is reduced per-op CPU overhead.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile from this new baseline; remaining likely targets are evalCompare/evalBinary and frame recycling/setup, plus other always-on checked helper calls.
asi.safety_note: Checked pop still exists for non-hot/general paths; correctness checks passed, so this remains semantics-preserving for valid emitted bytecode.

description: Reused singleton builtin FFI and module registries across VMs so New() no longer rebuilds immutable registry state for every interpreter instance.

asi.hypothesis: Builtin FFI and module registries are immutable after startup, so rebuilding them per VM is wasted work that affects every benchmarked program run.
asi.learning: This was another broad win. The suite dropped to ~761.0ms, decode improved again, and the bytecode microbenchmark fell to ~1.70ms/op with allocations down to ~144/op.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile from this new baseline. Remaining likely targets are evalCompare/evalBinary, newFrame/recycleFrame, and any remaining always-on object/int hot paths.
asi.safety_note: The shared registries are read-only after one-time initialization; correctness checks passed, so sharing them across VMs is safe in current usage.

description: Rejected unchecked push fast paths: some VM paths can legally operate with zero-capacity stacks, so bypassing the checked push caused index-out-of-range crashes.

asi.hypothesis: Mirroring the unchecked-pop win with unchecked pushes in hot verified opcode paths would remove the remaining capacity check overhead from successful execution.
asi.rollback_reason: The experiment crashed VM tests and the benchmark harness due to stack writes on frames whose backing stack length had not been grown yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep checked push semantics or redesign frame stack initialization before trying unchecked pushes again.
asi.error_detail: TestBytecodeWaitingOnFibers panicked with index out of range in pushUnsafe; autoresearch.sh then failed during hyperfine warmup.

description: Discarded lightweight child-VM spawning revisited after shared-registry wins: it still improved async-heavy paths somewhat, but not enough to beat the current suite best.

asi.hypothesis: Now that New() is cheaper and the baseline is lower, constructing child VMs directly in spawn() might finally clear the keep threshold on closure/fiber-heavy workloads.
asi.rollback_reason: The aggregate suite still did not beat the current best even though async-heavy slices improved, so the gain remains too narrow.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep focusing on costs paid by almost every opcode/function rather than closure-specific setup paths.
asi.learning: Child-VM spawn overhead is still secondary to the remaining always-on interpreter costs.

description: Added direct string and bool equality fast paths in evalCompare to avoid generic equality/type dispatch for common Eq/Neq cases.

asi.hypothesis: A noticeable share of remaining compare cost comes from common string/bool equality falling through generic Object.Equals; direct fast paths should shave that overhead without bloating the main dispatch loop.
asi.learning: This was a small but real suite-level win on top of the current best, with particularly nice fs/async/sql improvements while keeping the rest roughly flat.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile again; remaining broad opportunities are likely push/pop/newFrame and other tiny always-on interpreter costs rather than low-frequency helpers.
asi.confidence_note: The improvement is small, so future changes should be re-checked against noise carefully.

description: Stored pointers to canonical bytecode.Function entries in frames instead of copying function headers by value on every call, and routed method-call frame creation through newFrame.

asi.hypothesis: Frame creation still shows up hot; storing *bytecode.Function in Frame and centralizing method-call frame setup should reduce per-call copying and duplicated setup work.
asi.learning: This produced a solid broad win and materially improved sales/sql in particular. The suite dropped to ~751.7ms and the microbenchmark to ~1.65ms/op with lower bytes/op.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile from this new baseline; newFrame remains important, but more of its remaining cost may now be locals/stack slice handling rather than function-header copying.
asi.design_note: The change is semantics-preserving because functions are immutable within a loaded program, so frames can safely reference canonical function entries.

description: Expanded unchecked-pop usage across verified call/list/map/method/module/extern opcode paths so operand collection in realistic workloads avoids checked-pop overhead too.

asi.hypothesis: Unchecked pops should help more than just core arithmetic/compare paths; operand collection for calls, methods, collections, modules, and externs is also verifier-safe and frequent in the benchmark corpus.
asi.learning: This was a strong broad win. The suite dropped to ~735.9ms, with especially large gains in sales, decode, shape, and word-frequency. The microbenchmark also improved to ~1.62ms/op.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile from this baseline; remaining always-on costs are likely push, evalBinary/evalCompare, and residual frame setup/type work.
asi.safety_note: The change relies on verifier-guaranteed stack correctness for emitted bytecode; tests and checks passed.

description: Discarded moving frame cleanup from recycleFrame to newFrame: microbenchmark improved, but full-suite performance regressed overall versus the current best.

asi.hypothesis: Reused frames were being cleared twice (on recycle and on reuse); deferring cleanup to newFrame should reduce repeated frame housekeeping.
asi.rollback_reason: Despite a better microbenchmark, the full suite regressed versus the best state, likely because holding stale references until reuse increased real workload costs or shifted cleanup to worse points.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep immediate recycle cleanup and continue targeting always-on interpreter costs that show up in full-suite measurements.
asi.learning: Frame-lifecycle cleanup placement matters; the best microbenchmark timing does not guarantee the best realistic end-to-end behavior.

description: Rejected broad unchecked push fast paths: valid loop bytecode can exceed the pre-sized stack length assumptions in the current frame model, causing index-out-of-range panics.

asi.hypothesis: After unifying frame creation through newFrame, unchecked pushes across the VM loop might finally be safe and remove the remaining push branch overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: VM tests and autoresearch warmup crashed with index-out-of-range panics in pushUnsafe, including a for-loop test, so the current stack sizing invariants are not strong enough for unchecked pushes.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep checked push semantics unless stack sizing/invariants are strengthened first; focus on evalBinary/newFrame/type work instead.
asi.error_detail: TestBytecodeForLoop panicked in pushUnsafe with stack index out of range; autoresearch.sh then failed during hyperfine warmup.

description: Rejected direct frame-local argument filling for calls: the first pass broke closure-call operand ordering and caused widespread VM failures.

asi.hypothesis: Removing temporary args slice allocation/copying for direct, closure, and method calls by filling frame locals directly could cut remaining call-path overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The experiment broke VM semantics, especially OpCallClosure operand ordering, leading to many failures like 'expected closure, got int/string/map'.
asi.next_action_hint: If revisiting this idea, preserve the exact operand stack order for closure calls and rework one call path at a time with targeted tests.
asi.error_detail: Tests across decode/functions/modules failed after CallClosure started popping the closure object before argument collection.

description: Lazily initialized per-VM type caches so VMs that never resolve types avoid allocating a typeCache map up front.

asi.hypothesis: Even after shared registries, VM.New still allocates per-instance bookkeeping eagerly; deferring typeCache allocation until first real type resolution should shave a small but broad setup cost.
asi.learning: This was a modest but real full-suite win, especially helping decode/sales/sql while keeping the rest close. The microbenchmark improved too, confirming some residual VM setup overhead remained.
asi.next_action_hint: Re-profile from this new baseline; remaining broad hotspots are still evalCompare/evalBinary/push/pop and some residual frame/type work.
asi.confidence_note: This is another small structural setup win, so future micro-optimizations should still be checked carefully against suite noise.

description: Added direct int arithmetic and int comparison fast paths in the main VM opcode loop (OpAdd/Sub/Mul/Div/Mod and Eq/Neq/Lt/Lte/Gt/Gte) to bypass evalBinary/evalCompare dispatch for the hottest primitive case.

asi.hypothesis: A large remaining always-on cost is helper dispatch and kind-switching in evalBinary/evalCompare for int-int operations; inlining the int case directly in the opcode loop should reduce per-op CPU overhead broadly.
asi.learning: This produced a very large cross-suite win to ~711.9ms, with substantial improvements in decode, sales, shape, and word-frequency while preserving checks.
asi.safety_note: Fast paths only apply when both operands are ints and preserve existing semantics for other types by falling back to evalBinary/evalCompare.

description: Removed temporary argument-slice allocation on direct function calls by creating call frames from the caller stack and writing popped args straight into frame locals.

asi.hypothesis: Direct call paths still allocate a transient args slice per invocation; building the callee frame first and filling locals directly from stack pops should reduce call-heavy allocation/CPU overhead without semantic changes.
asi.learning: Another broad win (~700.8ms total). Decode, fs, and sql improved most while other workloads stayed stable or slightly better.
asi.safety_note: This optimization was limited to OpCall only; closure/method/extern call paths still use their prior argument handling, reducing semantic risk.
